paper_title: Quantum AI for Cancer Diagnostic Biomarker Discovery

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly defines Sample$_3$ as the combination of Sample$_1$ and Sample$_2$. Changing the subscript to $_2$ creates a contradiction where the text claims Sample$_2$ is the 'combined gene set' despite having just defined it as the 'Hypomethylated + Upregulated' set only.

The sentence establishes a mapping between Subtype-I/Subtype-II and the matrices Omic1.1/Omic1.2. Changing the first subscript to Subtype-II results in both matrices (1.1 and 1.2) being assigned to the same subtype, leaving Subtype-I without a corresponding matrix notation.

The text describes the threshold as 'stringent' and intended to identify 'statistically robust features' using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. In statistical hypothesis testing, significance is achieved when the p-value is less than or equal to the alpha level; flipping the sign to $\ge 0.05$ would select non-significant genes, contradicting the goal of identifying robust biomarkers.

The text explicitly defines the comparison as 'Tumor vs Normal'. The subscript TN is a standard abbreviation for Tumor-Normal used in the preceding step (1.1.1). Changing it to TT (Tumor-Tumor) creates an internal inconsistency with the verbal description of the comparison being performed.

The definition of a 'LUAD-specific' gene set should require the gene to NOT be significant in LUSC. Changing the condition for LUSC_TN from >= alpha (not significant) to < alpha (significant) contradicts the definition of specificity described in the text.

The text defines LUAD-specific hypermethylated genes as those upregulated in LUAD (U1) but not in LUSC (U2). Changing the set difference operator to a union includes genes upregulated in LUSC, which violates the 'LUAD-specific' requirement.

The text states that this discretization removes 'small, biologically irrelevant fold changes'. Changing the threshold from 0.1 to 1.5 is a massive shift that would exclude the majority of differentially expressed genes in a typical multiomic study, making the subsequent quantum training phase data-starved.

The text defines $S_{g,k}$ as representing 'Statistical Significance'. In standard statistical practice described in the paper (false discovery rate control), significance is achieved when the adjusted p-value is below a threshold (typically 0.05). Flipping the operator to '>' means the model would treat statistically insignificant results as significant.

The text defines significance in LUAD based on datasets F1 and F3. Using the min operator would require a gene to be significant in both datasets to be considered LUAD-significant, whereas the max operator (logical OR) correctly identifies a gene as significant if it appears in either relevant LUAD cohort.

perturbations.original:
\[
\begin{cases}
\text{LUAD-specific}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 0, \\
\text{Opposite both significant}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 1, \\
\text{Opposite weak}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 0 \text{ and/or } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 0.
\end{cases}
\]
perturbations.perturbed:
\[
\begin{cases}
\text{LUAD-specific}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 0, \\
\text{Opposite both significant}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 1, \\
\text{Opposite weak}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and/or } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 1.
\end{cases}
\]
perturbations.why_wrong: The definition of 'Opposite weak' is logically inconsistent with the other cases. If S_LUAD=1 and S_LUSC=1, it would fall under both 'Opposite both significant' and 'Opposite weak'. The context implies 'weak' should correspond to non-significance (S=0).

The text states that epsilon is used to avoid division by zero. Changing the sign to subtraction allows the denominator to become zero if the log term equals epsilon, defeating the stated purpose of the parameter.

The context describes imputing missing values for a feature using the median of that feature across samples. In the notation $X_{ij}$, $j$ typically represents the feature index and $i$ the sample index. Changing the subscript from $j$ to $i$ implies calculating the median across different features for a single sample (row-wise) rather than the median of the specific gene/feature across all samples (column-wise), which is inconsistent with standard imputation logic for genomic dataframes.
The text explicitly states that the data were split into 80% training and 20% testing. Setting test_size to 0.8 results in 20% training and 80% testing, contradicting the prose.
The paper defines X_DNA as having 128 features and X_RNA as having 128 features. The concatenation of these two feature sets must result in 256 features (128 + 128), not 128.

The text explicitly defines the F1-score as the 'harmonic mean' of precision and recall. The harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$ is $2ab/(a+b)$. Changing the constant from 2 to 1 makes the formula mathematically inconsistent with the definition of a harmonic mean provided in the preceding sentence.
